figures.caption: Table 1: Parent designs: main dimensions and hydrostatics.
Figure 1: (a) Renderings of the two parent hulls, Yacht A1 and Yacht A2. (b) A PCA plot of the final 140 unique geometries, colored by parent class.

Figure 2: The proposed multi-head architecture adapted from [18]. An input point cloud is processed by a shared regDGCNN backbone to produce a latent feature representation. This representation is then decoded by two specialized heads to predict the hull surface pressure and the 2D free surface wave map.

Figure 3: Learning-test curve summaries across ablation studies. (a–d) vary the number of points and training-data fraction; (e–h) vary grid resolution and loss-weight factors. Error bars denote 95% confidence intervals across 3 random seed variations. DS denotes Cp evaluated on down-sampled geometry points, while OG denotes Cp evaluated on the original full geometry, reconstructed via interpolation from the reduced point set, thereby capturing reconstruction error. The η curve shows wave elevation prediction performance.
Table 4: Architecture and training configuration (primary setting).
Figure 4: non-dimensionalized pressure (CP ) projected on the 2D ZY-Plane: truth (left), prediction (middle), absolute error (right) for bulb (bottom) and straight (top) bow variants.
Figure 5: Normalized 2D free-surface elevation (η): truth (left), prediction (middle), absolute error (right) for bulb (bottom) and straight (top) bow variants.
Figure 6: Qualitative loss ablation for the wave head: standard MSE leads to a trivial solution, while the hybrid loss improves structural fidelity.
Figure 8: Training and Validation Loss Curves.
Figure 9: 3D VTK representation visualized in ParaView.
